title: JWST Captures First Daily Cloud Cycle on Hot Jupiter WASP-94A b
subtitle: Webb's NIRSpec separated morning and evening atmospheres 700 light-years away. Silicate sand clouds form at dawn and vaporize above 1,000°C by dusk.
thumbnail_alt: Deep space nebula and stars photographed by space telescope
content_html:
Webb's NIRSpec instrument separated morning and evening atmospheres on WASP-94A b, 700 light-years away. Silicate sand clouds form at dawn and vaporize above 1,000 degrees Celsius by dusk, published in Science on May 21, 2026.

First Weather Cycle on an Exoplanet

WASP-94A b is tidally locked, meaning one side always faces its star. Webb isolated the two atmospheric limbs using transmission spectroscopy, revealing a daily cycle of cloud formation and vaporization that repeats every orbit.

What JWST Detected

The morning limb showed magnesium silicate cloud signatures. The evening limb showed those clouds had vaporized, replaced by clear high-temperature gas. Lead author Nathan Mayne of the University of Exeter called it the first direct evidence of a planet-wide weather system on a world outside our solar system.
